SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper gives a coherent technical argument that enabling recursion in an NPI-style architecture reduces the verification burden (Section 3.3) and presents empirical results on four tasks demonstrating perfect generalization for the recursive versions (Section 4). The verification procedure is described and applied to construct finite verification sets for each task, supporting their provable-generalization claims for those formulations. However, the theoretical treatment is informal rather than a formal proof paper (the ‘theorem’ in Sec. 3.3 is operationally argued but not formally proven for all tasks), and the experimental methodology lacks statistical rigor (very small training sets, single-implementation, no randomness/seeds or statistical tests, and limited baseline re-evaluation), plus some verification required changing the environment encoding (bubble sort) which is an additional assumption. These are minor but relevant issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but do limit how broadly the claims can be taken.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The central idea—explicitly incorporating recursion into neural programming architectures to obtain tractable, verifiable generalization—is a novel and clear perspective relative to prior NPI and neural-programming work, and the paper claims to be the first to combine recursion with a verification procedure (Introduction, Sec. 2). The instantiation (modifying NPI training traces to induce recursion) is a simple yet non-trivial engineering contribution, and applying it across addition, bubble sort, topological sort and quicksort shows breadth. While the idea builds on well-known recursion concepts and the existing NPI model, the combination and emphasis on provable generalization is an original contribution rather than a mere incremental tweak.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro, approach, NPI background, recursive formulations, verification, experiments, appendix) and provides detailed trace-generation algorithms and program sets in the appendix, which makes the methods reproducible in principle. Notation is consistent and examples (algorithms and trace fragments) are informative. There are occasional repetitious paragraphs and some formatting/typo artifacts in the provided text, and certain important assumptions (e.g., encoder modifications for verification) are buried in the appendix rather than highlighted earlier, which slightly reduces accessibility.
